headline: Bird by bird: only the next small unit
song:
Yo, Anne Lamott said it best—
Bird by bird, only the next small unit, forget the rest.
Bird by Bird... let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Bird by bird, that's the only way through,
One small unit, don't try to do the whole zoo.
You don't climb the mountain by staring at the peak,
You take the next step, then the next, stay meek.
Perfection's a trap, overwhelm's a lie,
Bird by bird—show up and try.
Stack the inches, ignore the sky,
Only the next unit till the day you die.
[Verse 1]
Her brother had a report on birds, school deadline looming,
Dad said, bird by bird, son—stop the dooming.
Lamott took that kitchen table truth and made a creed,
You don't write the novel; you write what you need.
One paragraph, one scene, one inch of the page,
Not the whole career staged on a rage.
Writers freeze 'cause they see the flock as one mass,
She says name the single bird and let the panic pass.
Short assignments: a one-inch picture frame on the desk,
Only what fits inside—that's the test.
Permission over perfection, progress over polish,
Finish the small unit, then demolish the wallish.
History of every book you love started with a line,
Not a perfect plan, not a star-aligned design.
She teaches: break the terror into units you can hold,
Bird by bird—that's how the story gets told.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: open the laptop, close the tabs,
One ticket in Jira, not the whole product's abs.
AI draft sitting there? Don't rewrite the universe tonight,
Fix one paragraph, ship one slide, keep it tight.
Side project? Don't build the empire this weekend,
Ship the landing page, one feature, one friend-end.
Kid's homework pile looks like a mountain of doom—
One worksheet, one problem, clear the room.
Gym app screaming 90-day transformation plans?
One set, one walk, bird by bird with your hands.
Slack full of threads? Answer one, then the next,
Inbox zero's a myth—claim the unit, protect the rest.
Doomscroll stopped when you name the next small act,
Close the feed, open Notes, write the fact.
Tomorrow: one unit on the calendar in ink,
Bird by bird is how overwhelm starts to shrink.
[Bridge]
The flock looks endless when you stand too far...
Step closer—one bird, one scar, one bar...
You don't need the whole sky to start...
Just the next small unit, honest heart.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So put the masterpiece down and pick up the next bird,
Name it out loud so the panic gets blurred.
Anne Lamott on the shelf, method in your bones,
One scene, one email, one call on your phone.
Permission over perfection—live that line tomorrow morning,
Before the coffee cools, start without warning.
Bird by bird is not cute—it's survival craft,
Only the next unit, then the next, then the draft.
Close the browser of everything you ain't done yet,
Open the one bird—and don't you forget.

headline: Shitty first drafts are required, not optional
song:
Yo, Anne Lamott said it best—
Shitty first drafts are required, not optional—pass the test.
Bird by Bird... let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Shitty first drafts, that's the only door in,
Ugly on purpose so the beauty can begin.
You don't get clean without getting dirty first,
Permission to suck is how the good gets nursed.
Required, not optional—say it out loud,
Let the bad draft breathe, drop the proud.
Write the mess, then dress the mess later,
First draft's a dump truck, not a curator.
[Verse 1]
Lamott named it plain: the shitty first draft is law,
Every writer she trusts still crawls through the raw.
You sit down thinking every sentence must shine,
She laughs—that's how the blank page wins every time.
Child's draft first: wild, wrong, free, full of trash,
Adult draft second: cut, shape, find the flash.
Third draft is the polish when you know what you mean,
But skip draft one and you're stuck in the machine.
She gives permission so the critic in your head
Can't veto the page before a word gets said.
History of published books is a graveyard of drafts,
Nobody mailed perfection—they mailed the after maths.
The secret club of writers isn't talent or fame,
It's people who let the first pass look lame.
Required means you don't earn the right to revise
Until the ugly version is breathing and alive.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: Cursor, ChatGPT, blank Notion page—
Generate the mess, don't curate the stage.
Post the ugly demo to the private Slack channel,
Ship v0.1 before you name the brand panel.
School essay? Dump the outline in pure freefall prose,
Then grade yourself after the truth shows.
Job application, portfolio, product brief—
Type the wrong version so the right can leaf.
Phone notes at 6 a.m.: three bad paragraphs, no delete,
Save as "DRAFT—DO NOT JUDGE," claim the seat.
TikTok creators who win still film takes that flop,
Writers who wait for perfect never drop.
Standup notes, OKRs, the slide deck for Monday—
Shitty first, then knife it on Sunday.
Required means you schedule the mess like a meeting,
Not optional vibes—calendar the bleeding.
[Bridge]
Ugly is a phase, not a verdict on you...
The draft is a room you walk through...
Shame wants silence; craft wants volume...
Write the trash—then find the jewel in the ruin.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So tomorrow morning: ten minutes, no backspace heroics,
Fill the page with wrong till it feels chaotic.
Anne said required—treat it like medicine, not mood,
Shitty first draft is how the real work gets glued.
Don't announce it, don't brand it, don't ask for applause,
Just finish the mess, then earn the edit laws.
Permission stamped: you are allowed to stink first,
Then revise till the true line bursts.
Open the doc. Title it SFD. Hit return—
Required draft done. Now you get to learn.

headline: Write what you can see from where you sit
song:
Yo, Anne Lamott said it best—
Write what you can see from where you sit—skip the rest.
Bird by Bird... let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Write what you can see from where you sit,
Don't fake the view you never hit.
Your window, your kitchen, your specific light,
Honest small truths beat borrowed insight.
From where you sit—the chair, the job, the scars,
That's the only map that still has stars.
Stop climbing other people's towers for a line,
See your seat. Write that. Make it mine.
[Verse 1]
Lamott teaches: you don't need Paris to write Paris,
You need the detail in front of you, not the status.
One-inch frame again—what's in the frame from your chair?
The chipped mug, the kid's backpack, the air.
False grandeur kills pages; false authority too,
Readers smell when you're faking a view.
She says start with your own kitchen table truth,
Family mess, church pew, suburban roof.
Great writers she loves still inventory the near—
Not the abstract universe, the cracked veneer.
Where you sit is not small; it's the only door,
Specific is universal when you dig the floor.
Stop waiting till life looks like a novel set,
The novel is already the life you get.
Observation over performance, presence over pose,
Write the seat you're in—that's how the real shows.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: close the travel porn tab,
Open Notes—describe the commute you actually have.
Remote job from a bedroom? Write the Wi-Fi hum,
Not some WeWork fantasy for the algorithm.
Parenting content without parenting the hour?
Write the 7 a.m. meltdown, not the influencer flower.
Side hustle from a phone? Document the real stack—
Stripe dashboard, unpaid invoice, midnight snack.
School essay on "community"? Don't Google a thesis first,
Walk your block, name three doors, quench the thirst.
AI can invent a skyline you never stood under—
Your job is the room you're actually in, no wonder.
LinkedIn polish is a costume; your seat is the source,
Pitch decks fail when they abandon course.
Twenty-five minutes: only what your eyes can own,
No posting, no flex—just the view from home.
[Bridge]
The chair is enough if you look hard...
Far places can wait; near things leave a scar...
Honesty starts at arm's length...
Write the sit. Trust the strength.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So restate it short: see your seat, write that view,
Run one block tomorrow where only that is true.
Anne Lamott's rule is a guardrail against fake deep,
From where you sit—that's the gold you keep.
Camera roll of your actual day, not the highlight reel,
Turn one still into sentences that feel.
No passport required, no perfect career arc,
Just the light on your desk after dark.
Sit down. Look left. Name three things without spin—
Write what you can see. Begin again.

headline: Perfectionism is the oppressor of living pages
song:
Yo, Anne Lamott said it best—
Perfectionism is the oppressor of living pages—no rest.
Bird by Bird... let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Perfectionism is the oppressor, freeze-frame thief,
Kills the living page to protect your self-belief.
It smiles like standards, acts like care,
But it chains the draft to a chair.
Living pages need breath, risk, and wrong,
Perfect wants silence where the song belongs.
Break the oppressor—let the ink stay loose,
Alive beats flawless. That's the truce.
[Verse 1]
Lamott names perfectionism as the voice of the inner mean,
Not excellence—control dressed clean.
It says wait until you're ready, wait until it's pure,
Meanwhile the living page dies of cure.
She learned: perfectionism is the enemy of the people
Who might have written if they weren't so fearful.
It polishes before there's anything to polish,
Edits the air, makes the writer abolish
Joy, play, the shitty draft, the risk of being seen—
All so the ego stays pristine.
Living pages have blood, jokes, uneven heat,
Dead pages have manners and nothing to eat.
Her book is a raid on the tyranny of neat,
Permission to be human on the sheet.
Oppressor means it rules by shame and delay,
Freedom is finishing imperfect today.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: name the habit that fights the living page—
Maybe ten rewrites of one email in a rage,
Maybe never hit send on the portfolio link,
Maybe AI polish till the voice goes extinct.
Cut that habit in half this week—literal half:
If you edit thrice, edit once; do the math.
Figma file stuck on pixel-perfect forever?
Ship the flow that works, fix later, be clever.
Instagram caption rewritten for an hour?
Post the true line; reclaim your power.
Code review theater while the feature waits dead?
Merge the useful, leave the gold-plate for ahead.
School paper? Stop refreshing Grammarly mid-sentence,
Finish the argument, then fix the tense.
Perfectionism loves tools that delay the done—
Your move is a timer and a "good enough" run.
[Bridge]
The oppressor whispers "not yet" in a velvet tone...
Living pages answer "already" alone...
Flawed and breathing beats sealed and still...
Choose the breath. Break the will.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So this week: half the polish habit, full the practice,
Anne's line on the sticky note where you can catch it.
Perfectionism is not your standards—it's your stall,
Living pages need a body that will fall
And get back up with ink on the hands.
Send one imperfect thing. That's the plan.
Oppressor loses when the draft goes public to one friend,
Or stays private but finished—either end.
Tomorrow: one living page, ugly and free—
Perfect can wait. Write for me.

headline: Character is want plus contradiction
song:
Yo, Anne Lamott said it best—
Character is want plus contradiction—pass the test.
Bird by Bird... let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Character is want plus contradiction, plain and deep,
What they hunger for, and the secret they keep.
Desire on the surface, a fracture underneath,
That's a person—not a puppet with clean teeth.
Want drives motion; contradiction makes them real,
Heroes who only win are cardboard, no feel.
Build them hungry, build them split,
Want plus contradiction—that's the kit.
[Verse 1]
Lamott on character: not a list of eye color and height,
Not a quirky hat—hunger and the fight.
What does this person want more than breath?
And where do they betray themselves to death?
Contradiction: kind and cruel, brave and small,
Faithful and fleeing, holy and fall.
She steals from life—people are never pure types,
They're knots of longing and opposing stripes.
Plot will come if the pressure hits a real need,
But first the soul must want and also bleed.
Flat characters want one thing and never flinch,
Round ones want love and still abandon the clinch.
Writers she trusts inventory the conflict inside,
Not the résumé—the private divide.
Want plus contradiction is the math of a self,
Everything else is costume on a shelf.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: your product user isn't "busy professional 30,"
They're want: control—and contradiction: doomscroll.
Founder story for the pitch? Don't sell pure grit myth,
Show the want for impact and the fear of being with
Failure, family, the quiet quit in your chest—
That's character investors actually invest.
AI roleplay for a novel? Prompt the split:
"Wants X, sabotages X, still can't quit."
Therapy journaling, team bios, brand voice guides—
Add the friction or the person dies.
Kid character in a game? Want: win the match,
Contradiction: afraid of being watched.
Dating app bio energy is all want, no crack—
Real connection starts when the cracks talk back.
Twenty-five minutes: write one person you know well,
Name their want, name the war they won't tell.
[Bridge]
Pure heroes bore the page to sleep...
Split souls make us lean and keep...
We recognize the fight we hide...
Want plus contradiction—inside.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So restate it short: people are hunger and a lie they live,
Run a block where only that tension gives.
Anne Lamott's character law is not for fiction alone,
It's how you see your boss, your kid, your own.
Tomorrow: sketch one figure—want clear, fracture named,
No posting—just the truth reclaimed.
If the page feels alive, you found the split,
If it feels like a brochure, you missed it.
Want plus contradiction—hold that dual flame,
Write the whole person. That's the game.

headline: Plot grows from pressure on character
song:
Yo, Anne Lamott said it best—
Plot grows from pressure on character—not a checklist.
Bird by Bird... let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Plot grows from pressure on character, heat on bone,
Not a map of beats you steal from a throne.
Squeeze the person, watch what they do next,
Story is reaction under stress, not a text.
Pressure, not puzzle pieces glued in a row,
Character under fire is how plots grow.
Turn the dial, don't invent empty twists,
Pressure on the soul—that's the list.
[Verse 1]
Lamott pushes back on outline worship that forgets the human,
Plot isn't a roller coaster designed for amusement.
It's what happens when a wanting, contradictory soul
Meets a force that won't let them stay whole.
Put them in a room with the thing they fear,
Or the thing they love with a cost too near.
She watches characters the way a parent watches kids under load—
Truth comes out when the easy road is closed.
History of stories that last: pressure, not fireworks alone,
Antigone, Gatsby, every throne of bone.
Formula without character is empty architecture,
She wants the architecture to serve the fracture.
Complications should matter to the want you named,
Otherwise the plot is just a game.
Grow the story by turning the screw on who they are,
Not by adding dragons from a jar.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: stop plotting the whole season of your startup,
Put the founder under real constraint—watch what pops up.
Product roadmap as a novel outline? Dead on arrival
Unless user pressure forces the survival.
Writer's block on the thriller? Don't buy a twist generator—
Raise the cost on the want; be the agitator.
AI can spit three-act structures by the pound;
Your job is pressure that makes the structure sound.
Parenting "plot" of the week: don't script the moral end—
Watch what your kid does when the plan won't bend.
Job search story: not a LinkedIn highlight reel,
Pressure of rent, pride, and what you'll actually feel.
Cut the habit of endless outlining with no heat—
Half the outline time, double the scene where they meet
A wall. Twenty-five minutes of pressure, not plan,
One scene where character pays for who they am.
[Bridge]
Maps are useful after you've walked the fire...
Pressure first—then the path gets higher...
Empty twists are sugar, not meat...
Squeeze the character. Feel the beat.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So this week: kill one habit that fights pressure with busywork,
Replace it with a scene where the want can't lurk.
Anne Lamott: plot grows when the person is pressed,
Not when the whiteboard is perfectly dressed.
Tomorrow: pick a character—yours or fiction's face—
Add one real cost and write the chase.
No fireworks required—just heat on the bone,
Pressure on character. Plot will grow on its own.
Close the beat sheet. Open the conflict.
Turn the screw. That's the gift.

headline: Turn off Radio KFKD—ego and self-loathing chatter
song:
Yo, Anne Lamott said it best—
Turn off Radio KFKD—ego and self-loathing, rest.
Bird by Bird... let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Turn off Radio KFKD, kill the static in the skull,
Ego on one dial, self-loathing on the full.
"You're a genius" / "You're garbage"—same broken station,
Both will wreck the work and steal the concentration.
Click the dial, mute the show, reclaim the quiet room,
Write without the broadcast of boom and doom.
KFKD is not truth—it's a circus of the mind,
Turn it off. Leave the noise behind.
[Verse 1]
Lamott names the station: K-Fucked, always on air,
One host says you're special beyond compare,
The other says you're talentless, fake, a fraud—
Same frequency, different fraud.
Neither voice is the work; both are theater of self,
And they drown the sentence that needs your help.
She teaches writers to notice when the radio spikes,
When comparison, shame, and grandiosity likes
Start spinning instead of the page under hand.
Discipline is not silence forever—it's command:
Hear it, name it, refuse to take orders from the host.
The page needs a quiet operator, not a ghost
Of applause or self-hate. Ego inflates then crashes,
Self-loathing pretends it's honest—both are ashes.
Turn the dial toward the task, toward the bird,
Toward the next true word.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: KFKD is now push notifications plus your head,
LinkedIn envy in the morning, doomscroll in bed.
"You're crushing it" posts and "everyone's ahead" feeds—
Same radio, two playlists of needs.
AI that flatters every draft is ego on steroids,
Comment sections that gut you are loathing's androids.
Phone-free hour titled "Radio Off"—calendar it real,
No metrics check while the first draft can heal.
Mute Slack status theater; hide the view count tab,
Write the thing that only exists if the noise can't grab
The wheel. Therapy apps help, but the rule is practical:
When the station gets loud, switch to tactical—
One paragraph, one commit, one dish washed clean,
Body in the present, not the fantasy screen.
Make one small finished thing that only lives
If KFKD is off—proof that quiet gives.
[Bridge]
Genius and garbage are both bad DJs...
The work needs a quieter frequency...
Name the station when it blares...
Then write like nobody cares.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So tomorrow: title the hour, shut the phone in a drawer,
Anne's radio rule is a door.
When "I'm the best" or "I'm nothing" starts the show,
Smile, label it KFKD, and let it go.
Ego and self-loathing chatter don't get the mic,
The page does—short, honest, like.
One finished small thing: a note, a scene, a plan on paper,
Proof you can live without the vapor.
Turn it off. Keep it off while the unit gets done—
Then live your life. Station's gone.

headline: Publication is not the point of practice
song:
Yo, Anne Lamott said it best—
Publication is not the point of practice—confess.
Bird by Bird... let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Publication is not the point of practice, hear that clear,
The practice is the life—the printed thing is gear.
You don't grow a soul by chasing a deal alone,
You show up to the page and make it home.
Published is weather; practice is climate and bone,
If you only write for yes, you'll write for loan.
The point is attention, courage, return tomorrow,
Not the spine on a shelf to borrow.
[Verse 1]
Lamott has been published and still says the quiet part:
Fame doesn't fix the fear or heal the heart.
Writers who treat acceptance letters as the only score
Burn out, sell out, or write less than before.
Practice is the daily seat, the bad draft, the bird,
The attention to your life that writing spurred.
She knows the industry: agents, advances, the lottery myth,
And still points back to the desk and the grit.
Publication can be joy, money, readers, a door—
It's not the reason you learn to endure.
If the only point is print, the work becomes a costume,
Practice dies when the market is the only costume.
She offers a longer game: become a person who writes,
Not a person who waits for lights.
The point is formation—how you see, how you stay,
Publication is optional weather on that day.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: not just books—posts, launches, follower counts,
"Published" is any applause metric that mounts.
Stop making the TikTok post the reason you practice the craft,
Practice the craft so the post is an after, not the raft.
Side project only "counts" if Product Hunt loves it? Lie.
Ship for skill: one hour phone-free, titled why—
"Publication not the point"—show up even if mood is gone,
Mood is not the contract; the seat is the bond.
School portfolios, GitHub stars, newsletter open rates—
Useful, not sacred. Don't let them dictate
Whether you practice on a quiet Tuesday night.
Book the hour. Protect the hour. Dim the light.
AI can "publish" infinite content in a flash;
Your practice is human attention you don't trash.
Unfollow the scoreboard for sixty minutes straight,
Write or make for the sake of staying awake.
[Bridge]
Applause is sugar; practice is bread...
One feeds the ego, one keeps you fed...
Weather changes; the desk remains...
Show up. That's the gain.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So book one phone-free hour with Anne's line as the name,
Show up even if the mood refuses the flame.
Publication is not the point—say it till it's muscle,
Practice is the point when the metrics hustle.
Tomorrow: sixty minutes, no audience required,
Just the work that keeps you wired.
If something publishes later, fine—celebrate then,
But don't mortgage today for a maybe amen.
Close the analytics. Open the practice.
That's the point. Hold the axis.

headline: One trusted reader beats a chorus
song:
Yo, Anne Lamott said it best—
One trusted reader beats a chorus—skip the rest.
Bird by Bird... let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
One trusted reader beats a chorus of noise,
A hundred opinions will scatter your poise.
Find the person who loves the work and tells the truth,
Not the crowd that claps or cuts for proof.
Chorus can cheer and chorus can kill,
One clear friend with craft and will.
Trusted over trending, depth over volume's reign,
One reader. That's the lane.
[Verse 1]
Lamott warns: workshop culture can drown a draft in voices,
Every note pulls a different set of choices.
You end up writing by committee, self erased,
A average of fears, a product paste.
She wants a reader who is for your book and for the truth—
Not a fan who only flatters youth,
Not a rival who only cuts to win,
Someone who can say "this part is thin"
And still believe the whole might live.
That's rare. That's the gift you give
Yourself when you stop crowdsourcing the soul.
Writing groups have value; chaos has a toll.
She learned to protect the early draft from the mob,
And later open the door with a smarter job:
One or few, chosen, honest, kind and sharp,
Not a chorus that turns the page into a tarp.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: comments sections are a chorus from hell,
Design critiques with twenty people and no spell.
Slack "thoughts?" to the whole company freezes the product,
One trusted design partner keeps it conductive.
AI will give you fifty takes in a second flat—
Still pick one human who knows where you're at.
Writing Discord with fifty feedback threads? Filter hard,
One reader who finishes and marks the scarred
Parts with care. Portfolio reviews on Twitter spaces
Can be a storm—choose the face that actually traces
Your intent. Code review: one senior who ships with you
Beats a pile-on of drive-by "nit: rename the foo."
Make one small finished thing that only exists
If you trusted one reader—not the viral lists.
Send the draft to them. Not the feed. Not the mob.
One voice. Do the job.
[Bridge]
Crowds are weather; trust is climate...
Too many maps and you never climb it...
Choose the friend who can hold both love and knife...
That's how drafts get a life.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So name your one reader this week—text them tonight,
Ask for honesty, not hype and light.
Anne Lamott's rule protects the living page from noise,
One trusted reader beats a chorus of toys.
Finish something small that needs that single eye,
Revise from their truth, not the sky.
Mute the group chat for the early draft phase,
Open the door later, on your days.
One reader. Real notes. Real rewrite—
Chorus can wait. Get it right.

headline: Writing is training in attention to life
song:
Yo, Anne Lamott said it best—
Writing is training in attention to life—not a contest.
Bird by Bird... let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Writing is training in attention to life, eyes awake,
Not just sentences—how you take the day you make.
Notice the light, the kid, the bus, the bruise, the joke,
Attention is the muscle; the page is the stroke.
You don't write to escape the world for long—
You write to see it true and belong.
Train the gaze, then the words will follow through,
Attention to life—that's the school.
[Verse 1]
Lamott's deepest offer isn't tips for agents and deals,
It's a way of being awake to what is real.
Writing forces you to look twice at the ordinary scene,
To name the shame, the grace, the in-between.
Attention is love's cousin; distraction is the thief,
Her practice is a raid on the numb belief
That life is background for your ambition show.
Sit, notice, render—let the small things grow
Large enough to hold meaning without fake depth.
She writes about faith, family, death, and breath
As training: keep showing up to what is actually here.
Craft is secondary to the clear.
When you train attention, plot and character get fed,
Because you stopped sleepwalking through what was said.
Writing as spiritual practice without the pretension—
Just honest eyes and a pen's intention.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: attention is the scarce resource of the 2020s,
Phones farm it; writing is how you seize
It back. Twenty-five minutes, no posting, restated line:
"Writing trains attention to life—make it mine."
Walk without earbuds once; name five sounds you missed,
Then write them flat—no poetry for the list.
Dinner with family: put the phone in another room,
Notice who speaks, who folds, who lights the gloom.
Work Slack is a slot machine for half-attention—
One deep block of seeing beats a day of mentions.
AI can summarize your life into bullet points thin;
Only you can attend to the weather on your skin.
Journaling apps help if you use them to look, not perform,
Not for streaks— for the form of the storm.
School, parenting, product: same drill every day—
What did you actually see? Write that, no display.
[Bridge]
The feed wants your eyes; the page wants your soul...
Attention is the toll you pay to be whole...
Look longer than the scroll allows...
Then write the now.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So restate it short: writing trains how I see my days,
Run one block that obeys—no posts, no praise.
Anne Lamott: the craft is a school for living awake,
Not a ladder to a brand you fake.
Tomorrow: twenty-five minutes of pure notice then ink,
One scene from your real life—think, then link.
Training means reps; attention means return,
Bird by bird, the eyes learn.
Close the app. Open the window. Then the page—
Attend to life. That's the stage.

headline: Stock the writer’s toolbox by reading widely
song:
Yo, Stephen King said it best—
Stock the writer's toolbox by reading widely—no rest.
On Writing... let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Stock the toolbox by reading widely, fill the kit,
Vocabulary, cadence, courage—tools you get
From other books, not from wishing hard alone,
You build the box by what you put in the bone.
Read widely—trash and treasure, high and street,
Every page is a wrench you might need to meet.
Empty toolbox, empty pages, that's the law,
Stock it full. Then you draw.
[Verse 1]
King's metaphor is plain: a writer carries a toolbox,
Common tools on top—vocab, grammar, style that unlocks.
Deeper down: the narrative sense, the ear for truth,
You don't buy those at a store in your youth.
You stock them by reading—a lot, and wide as weather,
Bad books teach what not to do; good books tether
You to possibility. He read everything he could grab,
Not to copy voice, but to expand the lab.
Imitation is a phase; absorption is the long game,
Your toolbox is the sum of the pages you claim.
He rejects the myth of pure talent with no input stream,
Craft is equipment; reading is how you redeem
The blank. Strunk and White on the upper tray, sure—
But novels, newspapers, the whole allure.
Stock means continuous, not a weekend course,
Widely means outside your comfort's force.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: name the habit that fights the stock—
Maybe only feeds, only same-genre lock,
Maybe "I'll read when I finish my own book" forever,
Cut that habit in half this week—be clever.
Audible on the commute, paper before bed,
One book outside your lane to stretch the head.
AI summaries are not stocking the toolbox deep—
They're CliffsNotes while the tools stay asleep.
Engineers read codebases; writers read books that breathe,
Product folks read users; same principle underneath.
TikTok book recs are maps—still open the actual text,
Highlight one tool: a dialogue turn, a structure flexed.
Half the scrolling time becomes twenty pages a night,
Toolbox gets heavier, prose gets light.
Read the "trash" without shame if it teaches pace,
Read the hard stuff without pride if it teaches grace.
[Bridge]
An empty box can't fix a broken line...
You stock in silence over time...
Widely means curiosity over brand...
Fill the kit. Then stand.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So this week: cut the anti-reading habit in half,
Put a book where the phone usually laughs.
Stephen King: toolbox first, then the work can bite,
Stock by reading widely—start tonight.
One novel, one essay, one thing you'd never pick,
Note one tool you stole without the theft trick—
Transform it tomorrow in your own draft's need,
That's the point of the seed.
Fill the box. Carry it. Use what you stocked—
Read widely. Unlock.

headline: Read a lot and write a lot—no substitute pair
song:
Yo, Stephen King said it best—
Read a lot and write a lot—no substitute for the pair.
On Writing... let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Read a lot, write a lot—no substitute pair,
Not talent alone, not a course, not a prayer.
Volume of pages in and pages out,
That's the gym where real writers shout.
Skip one half and the other half dies,
Input and output—twin supplies.
No magic app replaces the stack and the seat,
Read a lot. Write a lot. Repeat.
[Verse 1]
King's most famous law is almost rude in its simplicity:
There is no substitute for the dual electricity
Of constant reading and constant writing—years, not moods.
Workshops help; theory helps; but the food
Is pages. He wrote a lot even when the checks were small,
Read a lot even when the world called it a stall.
Talent without volume is a spark without wood,
Volume without reading is a wheel without good
Direction. The pair is non-negotiable, not a slogan—
It's how the ear learns and the hand stays open.
He measures seriousness by the hours and the books,
Not by the bio lines or the looks.
If you want the job, do the job's two halves daily,
Even when inspiration is failing you gaily.
No substitute means stop hunting the secret third thing,
The pair is the secret. Start the swing.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: book one phone-free hour titled the pair,
Show up even if the mood isn't there.
Half the hour read, half the hour write—or split the week,
But don't let "content" replace books while you speak
Of being a writer. Podcasts are gravy; books are meat,
Tweets are not a diet of complete
Sentences. AI can draft while you "supervise"—
That's not write a lot; that's a disguise.
Commute: audiobook. Lunch: ten pages. Night: five hundred words,
King's own targets were volume, not pretty birds.
School, job, kids: the pair shrinks but never dies—
Fifteen minutes each still multiplies.
Cut the substitute hunt: another course, another thread,
Another productivity system instead
Of the chair. Calendar the pair like a meeting with pay,
Protect it from Slack and the endless scroll day.
[Bridge]
Mood is a liar; volume is a friend...
The pair doesn't care how you feel in the end...
Read to stock; write to spend the stock...
No third trick. Tick the clock.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So tomorrow: phone-free hour, title on the calendar bold,
Read a lot, write a lot—do what King told.
No substitute pair—say it when the shortcut calls,
When the course ad sings and the feed enthralls.
Stack of pages in, stack of pages out,
That's the path without the doubt.
Show up when the mood disappears into gray,
The pair works anyway.
Close the course tab. Open the book. Then the doc—
Read. Write. No substitute. Lock.

headline: Write with the door closed; rewrite with it open
song:
Yo, Stephen King said it best—
Write with the door closed; rewrite with it open—confess.
On Writing... let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Write with the door closed—first draft is private ground,
No audience, no committee, no crowd around.
Rewrite with it open—now invite the light,
Other eyes, hard cuts, make it right.
Closed for honesty; open for craft that can stand,
Two modes, one story, both in your hand.
Don't mix them up or the draft will choke,
Door closed. Door open. That's the stroke.
[Verse 1]
King's door is literal and spiritual both:
First draft, shut the world out—protect the growth
Of a story that only you can hear at first.
If you write for the market mid-sentence, you burst
The bubble of truth. Closed door means for yourself,
Tell the tale as it wants, not for the shelf
Yet. Then open door rewrite: now it's for them,
The Ideal Reader, the clarity gem.
Cut what only you loved, keep what communicates,
Invite criticism when the structure tolerates
It. He separates creation from communication in time,
So neither phase pollutes the other's climb.
Closed is freedom; open is responsibility and steel,
Both required for a book that's real.
Skip closed and you perform; skip open and you rant,
King wants the whole dance—not one half of the chant.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: closed door is DND on Slack, phone in another room,
Doc named PRIVATE—no share link, no Zoom.
Twenty-five minutes: only generate, no posting, no "thoughts?"
Open door later: one trusted reader, real shots.
Google Docs with twenty editors live is open too early,
Kills the closed-door weirdness that makes stories surly
And true. AI co-writer in the first pass can be a crack
In the door—use it after you know your track.
Startup pitch: write the ugly version alone at night,
Open door rewrite with the team in the light.
School essay: draft without the rubric in your face,
Then open the rubric and cut with grace.
"Building in public" is open door energy—great for polish,
Dangerous if it colonizes the first-draft abolish
Of privacy. Schedule both modes on the calendar clear:
Closed block. Open block. No blur here.
[Bridge]
Closed door: tell the truth you fear...
Open door: make that truth land here...
Two rooms, one house, don't collapse the wall...
Write closed. Rewrite open. That's all.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So restate it short: draft alone, revise for the world,
Run one closed block tomorrow—flag unfurled
On the door. No audience. Then later, open and cut.
Stephen King's two-door rule is the gut
Check for every modern share-everything habit.
Protect the first pass from the rabbit
Of approval. Then earn the open light with work that's ready.
Door closed. Door open. Steady.
Hit save on the private draft. Breathe. Then invite—
Rewrite in the light.

headline: Kill darlings that do not serve the story
song:
Yo, Stephen King said it best—
Kill darlings that do not serve the story—no rest.
On Writing... let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Kill your darlings if they don't serve the story's need,
Pretty lines that stall the plot still need to bleed.
Love is not enough—utility is the test,
If it shines but doesn't serve, put it to rest.
Darlings die so the whole can live clean,
Cut the precious, keep the mean.
Serve the story, not your favorite toy,
Kill the darling. Keep the joy of the true deploy.
[Verse 1]
King quotes the old advice and lives it with a knife:
Your favorite sentence can still wreck the life
Of a chapter. Darling means you love it more than the tale,
It's a vanity mirror in the middle of the trail.
He kills adverbs, kills digressions, kills the clever bit
That makes readers trip. Serve means fit
The narrative pressure, character, pace, and truth.
If it only serves your ego, that's the proof
It has to go. Not because beauty is bad—
Because misplaced beauty is a fad
That costs momentum. History of strong books is a graveyard of cuts,
Pages that were good alone but closed the ruts
Of story. Honesty over attachment—that's the ethic,
Revision is a kind of medical.
Kill means cut or move or bury in a file of later maybe,
Not hate your craft—just free the baby
From ornaments that don't feed it.
Serve the story. That's the edict.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: kill the feature users don't need but founders love,
Kill the slide with the perfect chart that doesn't prove
The point. Kill the LinkedIn paragraph of flex in the proposal,
Kill the AI-polished metaphor that makes the email fossil
Of clarity. Design darlings: the animation that delays the task,
Code darlings: the clever abstraction that hides the ask.
Twenty-five minutes: open a draft and hunt one darling by name,
Ask "does this serve?"—if no, kill without shame.
School essay: the quote you love that doesn't support the claim—
Out. Portfolio piece that's pretty but fake—
Out or rework. Songwriters: the hook that doesn't fit the verse,
Product: the onboarding confetti that makes users worse.
Save darlings in a graveyard doc if you must feel safe,
But don't leave them in the road as a waif.
Cut half the precious this week; feel the story speed up,
That's the high of the cleanup.
[Bridge]
Attachment is a fog on the edit eye...
Service is the clear sky...
What you love can still be wrong for the whole...
Kill it. Save the soul.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So restate it short: cut what I love if it doesn't serve,
Run a block of murder with a steady nerve.
Stephen King: darlings that stall the story must die,
Not maybe—apply.
Tomorrow: one draft, one darling, one hard delete or move,
Feel the page improve.
Keep a scrap file for the ego if you need the soft landing,
But the story gets the standing.
Serve the story. Wield the knife. Be kind to the whole—
Kill the darling. Make it whole.

headline: Story is situation plus complication
song:
Yo, Stephen King said it best—
Story is situation plus complication—pass the test.
On Writing... let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Story is situation plus complication, clean and raw,
A guy in a place, then something breaks the law
Of ease. Not a theme first, not a moral on a stick—
Situation, then the hitch that makes it tick.
What if? Then what next? Pressure on the scene,
Complication turns a snapshot into a machine.
Keep it simple at the root, then twist the screw,
Situation plus complication—true.
[Verse 1]
King strips story down: you don't start with "my book is about trauma,"
You start with people in a situation—drama
Arrives when complication enters like weather.
A writer in a room; a car on a road; two strangers together—
Then the thing goes wrong, or the secret opens, or the need
Collides with a wall. That's the seed.
He distrusts plotting that invents the end before the walk,
Prefers to put characters in a spot and let them talk
Under stress. Situation is the setup you can see;
Complication is the force that won't let them be
Free. Theme comes later, after honest telling,
Not as a lecture you're selling.
This is the engine under Carrie, under Misery's room—
A clear situation, then the dark bloom
Of what if. Students overcomplicate with structure charts;
King says start with the parts: place, person, spark.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: your pitch deck "story" is situation plus heat—
User has a job; complication makes the tool elite.
Not "we empower synergy"—situation: invoice late,
Complication: cash flow breaks, then your product's fate.
Novel with AI help? Prompt situation first, then twist,
Don't prompt "write a three-act masterpiece" into the mist.
Game design: player in a level, then the resource dies,
That's story. Meeting narrative: team in a bind, then the surprise
Constraint from legal. Parenting: kid wants X, world says no—
Situation plus complication—watch them grow.
Twenty-five minutes: write one situation in five lines flat,
Add one complication that matters—sit with that.
No theme paragraph yet. No moral. Just the screw turning,
Then see what characters start learning.
Cut the habit of outlining endings before the room is real,
Half the ending obsession; double the situational feel.
[Bridge]
Theme is smoke; situation is fire...
Complication is the wind that takes it higher...
Start with the room, not the sermon...
Then twist. That's the sermon.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So restate it short: put someone somewhere, then make it hard,
Run a block that obeys—no theme card.
Stephen King: story is situation plus complication's weight,
Not a TED talk you narrate.
Tomorrow: one page—situation clear, complication true,
No posting. Just the two.
If it moves, you found story; if it lectures, start again,
Back to the room, back to the when.
Situation. Complication. Then the rest will follow through—
Keep it honest. That's the glue.

headline: Description is observation in service of story
song:
Yo, Stephen King said it best—
Description is observation in service of story—invest.
On Writing... let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Description is observation in service of story, not a tour,
See clear, pick what matters, leave the rest on the floor.
Not a catalog of curtains for the ego to display—
Details that work, that pull the scene your way.
Observe like a camera, edit like a knife,
Serve the mood, the character, the life.
Too much paint and the story drowns in gloss,
Observation with a job—that's the boss.
[Verse 1]
King hates lazy description and hates purple piles equal,
He wants you to see, then choose the sequel
Of which detail earns the page. Observation first—
Honest eyes—then service: does this detail burst
With meaning for the moment? Weather that mirrors nothing
Is weather for weather. A scar that tells something
Is gold. He learned description by reading and looking hard,
Not by dumping thesaurus cards.
"Don't tell me the moon is shining; show me the glint of light
On broken glass"—the spirit, if not the exact cite,
Lives in his approach: concrete, selective, alive.
Description that serves character and drive
Beats description that shows you did homework on chairs.
Every object on the page should pay its fares.
Observation without service is a travel blog insert,
Service without observation is a blur that hurts.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: each morning for seven days, read King's line,
Then in under two minutes, name one detail that earns time—
In your commute, your standup, your product UI state.
Write it once. Serve a story: what does it dictate
About mood or stakes? Phone camera roll is observation raw;
Your job is to pick the frame that has claw.
AI can describe a room in fifty adjectives flat—
You choose the three that make the conflict spat.
UX writing: describe the empty state that serves the user need,
Not poetry for the brand's greed.
Game environments: detail that guides, not clutter for prestige,
Slack updates: one observed fact that moves the siege.
Cut the habit of decorative worldbuilding dumps this week,
Half the adjectives; double the details that speak.
TikTok "day in the life" is often observation without service—
Add why the detail matters; that's the purchase.
[Bridge]
Eyes open, hand selective...
Every detail a detective...
Service keeps the page moving forward...
Observe. Choose. Move toward.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So seven mornings: read the line, two-minute observation drill,
One detail in service—build the skill.
Stephen King: description isn't decoration for the shelf,
It's story-working stealth.
Tomorrow: describe a real room with three serving details only,
Feel the scene less lonely
And more sharp. Cut the rest to a note file for later maybe,
Keep the page from getting gravy.
Observe. Serve. Leave the catalog behind—
Description with a mind.

headline: Dialogue is character revealed by speech
song:
Yo, Stephen King said it best—
Dialogue is character revealed by speech—no less.
On Writing... let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Dialogue is character revealed by speech, not chat filler,
How they talk is who they are—that's the thriller.
Rhythm, dodge, vocabulary, what they won't say,
Speech is a fingerprint on the day.
If everyone sounds like you, the cast is a mask,
Let them speak till the difference is a task.
Reveal by speech—conflict in the mouth,
Character talks. Truth comes out.
[Verse 1]
King treats dialogue as a primary tool of revelation,
Not a vehicle for plot facts and explanation
Dumps. People reveal class, fear, education, love,
In the music of how they shove
Words around. He listens—real talk is irregular, alive,
Full of subtext, interruption, the drive
To hide and to confess at once. Bad dialogue is samey,
On-the-nose, TED-talking the theme lazy.
Good dialogue can carry a scene with almost no description
Because the voices do the depiction.
He wants you to hear the difference between two humans under stress,
Not two mouthpieces in a dress
Of plot. Read your dialogue aloud—ear is the judge,
If it clanks, don't fudge.
Character is want plus history plus lie; speech is where it leaks,
That's the technique he seeks.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: twenty-five minutes—write two people who want opposite things,
Only dialogue, no stage directions with wings.
If you can't tell them apart, you failed the reveal,
Fix vocabulary, pace, what they conceal.
Product research interviews: listen for speech patterns of pain,
Not just the feature request in the rain.
AI script help often makes everyone sound like a podcast host—
Push back; force the difference; don't coast.
Team Slack is dialogue revealing culture—read the subtext in "per my last,"
Write a scene from a real meeting that just passed.
Game NPCs: one speech tell per character, consistent and earned,
Dating texts: what's revealed when the tone is burned.
School plays and podcasts: cut the exposition monologue,
Put the fact in conflict talk, not a blog.
Restate: speech reveals who—they are under heat,
Run the block. No posting. Just the beat of feet
In the mouth. Record yourself reading both parts out loud,
Fix what sounds like a crowd of one.
[Bridge]
Same voice everywhere is a costume party flat...
Difference is character—listen for that...
Subtext over sermon in every line...
Speech as spine.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So restate it short: how they talk is who they are under fire,
Run one dialogue-only scene; take it higher.
Stephen King: dialogue is character revealed by speech—
Not small talk on the beach
Of filler. Tomorrow: two voices, one conflict, zero same tone,
Make the difference known.
Read it aloud. Cut the line that only explains the plot,
Keep the line that shows the knot.
Character speaks. You listen. Then you write what you hear—
Reveal. Clear.

headline: Theme emerges from honest storytelling in revision
song:
Yo, Stephen King said it best—
Theme emerges from honest storytelling in revision—bless.
On Writing... let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Theme emerges—don't force it on page one like a brand,
Tell the honest story first, then understand
What it was about in revision's light.
Meaning grows from truth, not from a fight
To sound deep. Honest first, theme second-born,
Revision is where the pattern is worn
Into clarity. Don't preach the poster before the tale,
Let theme emerge. Don't jail
The story in a slogan too soon.
Honest storytelling. Revision. Tune.
[Verse 1]
King pushes against starting with "my theme is revenge and capitalism,"
Then stuffing characters into that prism.
Story first: situation, people, complication, honest heat—
After draft two or three, you see the beat
Of what you were really saying. Theme is discovered,
Not installed like a server.
Revision is the laboratory where accidents of meaning
Become structure: motifs, cuts, the cleaning
Of false starts that diluted the true concern.
He trusts the subconscious if you tell true; you learn
In the rewrite what the book already knew.
Forced theme is a school essay with a plot glued
On top. Emergent theme is a novel that haunts,
Because the honesty was never for points.
Write true, then read yourself like a critic who cares,
Theme will be there—if you dare
To cut what doesn't belong to what emerged.
That's the purge.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: name the habit that fights emergence—
Maybe thesis-first outlines that kill the surge,
Maybe LinkedIn carousels of "5 lessons" before the work exists,
Cut that habit in half; let the draft insist.
Startup "narrative" often starts with theme ("we democratize X")
Before customer situation is fixed—
Flip it: honest user stories first, theme of the brand later
From patterns in the data.
AI will gladly spit a theme paragraph on command;
Your job is a draft that feels like land,
Then a revision pass titled "what is this actually about?"
Highlight recurring images; cut the devout
Speeches that never earned their sermon.
School essays can still outline—but creative work? Determine
Theme after a true draft exists. Podcast episodes too:
Tell the story, then name the glue
In the edit. Half the pre-theme preaching this week,
Double the honest scene work. Feel the theme speak
Without a billboard.
[Bridge]
Posters before stories are empty rooms...
Revision finds the ghost that looms...
Honesty is the soil; theme is the tree...
Don't plant the tree in air. Let it be.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So this week: cut the force-theme habit in half, practice On Writing's pace,
Honest story first, theme in the second place—
Emergent in revision. Tomorrow: finish a true messy scene,
Then ask what it means—clean
With cuts, not with a slogan stuck on top.
Stephen King: let theme drop
Out of the work like ore from rock you actually mined.
Don't fake the find.
Tell it true. Revise awake. Name the pattern last—
Theme emerges. Hold fast.

headline: A room and a door that shuts are requirements
song:
Yo, Stephen King said it best—
A room and a door that shuts are requirements—not a guest.
On Writing... let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
A room and a door that shuts—requirements, not a luxury flex,
You need a place the world can't text.
Closed door is a contract with the work and the self,
Without it, craft stays on the shelf.
Not a mansion—just a boundary that holds,
Space and silence where the story unfolds.
Requirements mean non-negotiable gear,
Room. Door. Shut. Here.
[Verse 1]
King is practical: tools of the trade include a space,
A door you can close so the mind has a place
To go deep. He's not romanticizing the garret myth alone—
He's saying interruption is a thief of tone
And courage. Early in a career, the room might be small,
A laundry corner, a hour after kids in the hall—
But the shut door is the principle: protect the seat.
Without physical or temporal walls, the work will retreat
Into "someday." He wrote with kids in the house and still
Fought for the closed-door will.
Requirements are few: something to write with, something to write on,
And a door. Everything else is optional con.
The door trains others and trains you—this hour is claimed,
Not available for every name
That knocks. Seriousness looks like architecture of attention,
A room is a decision, not pretension.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: name the habit that fights the shut door—
Maybe open-plan life, phone face-up on the floor
Of the desk, Slack green all day, AirPods as fake walls.
Cut that habit in half this week—real calls:
Book a room, a library carrel, a car in a lot,
A closet with a lamp if that's what you got.
Door can be digital: Focus mode, app blockers, a sign on the knob,
Partner agreement: this hour is the job.
Coworking without headphones is not a shut door,
"Work from bed with TikTok" is a trap door.
Remote teams: put DND on the calendar with teeth,
Not a vibe—a requirement underneath.
Side project dies in the living room scroll more than from lack of talent,
Build the room. Be gallant
About defending it. Half the "I'll write when it's quiet" waiting—
Make quiet. Stop debating.
[Bridge]
The door is a promise you keep with your hands...
Space is how seriousness stands...
Not fancy—firm...
Shut it. Confirm.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So this week: claim one room and one shut—literal or timed,
Cut the interruption habit that climbed
Into your hours. Stephen King: requirements, not mood-based hope,
Door closed is how you cope
With a noisy century. Tomorrow: sixty minutes behind a real barrier,
No negotiation, no carrier
Of pings. Write or read. Be unavailable on purpose.
Room. Door. Shut. Surface
The work. Then open when the unit is done—
Requirement met. You've won the first one.

headline: Writing is telepathy—transmit clear images
song:
Yo, Stephen King said it best—
Writing is telepathy—transmit clear images—no guess.
On Writing... let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Writing is telepathy—mind to mind across the years,
You put a picture here; they see it there, no mirrors.
Transmit clear images, concrete and bright,
Not fog of abstraction, not purple night.
If they see the table, the key, the red carpet square,
You did the magic—telepathy rare.
Clear over clever, image over haze,
Transmit. Make them gaze.
[Verse 1]
King opens with the table, the cage, the red cloth—famous scene:
He describes; you see; distance collapses between
Writer and reader. That's telepathy without the mystic tax—
Shared imagination on the tracks
Of precise language. He insists writing's job is transfer,
Not to impress with how you can refer
To concepts. Clear images beat vague "emotional energy" every time,
Show the thing so their brain can rhyme
With yours. The reader collaborates—fills gaps you leave—
But you must send a signal they can receive.
Fuzzy writing is failed telepathy, not deep style,
It's static on the line. He walks a mile
To make you see what he sees; that's respect and craft,
Description and dialogue in service of that draft
Of shared sight. Telepathy is the metaphor for communication pure,
Everything else is literature's lure
Toward ego. Transmit first. Flourish later if it still serves.
Clear images. Nerve.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: each morning for seven days, read King's line,
Then in under two minutes, send one image—sharp, not fine
Art—just clear. A coffee ring, a Slack red badge count,
A kid's untied shoe—make the picture mount
In someone else's head. Product copy: show the before/after scene,
Not "seamless synergy" machine.
AI loves abstract nouns; your job is to force the concrete,
Rewrite until a stranger can complete
The picture. TikTok thrives on image; writing can too—
One visual per paragraph as a rule of thumb for you.
Pitch decks: one slide, one image of the user stuck,
Not a word cloud of luck.
Text a friend a three-sentence scene; ask what they saw—
If they miss, you failed telepathy law.
Cut jargon half this week; double the sensory fact,
Feel the signal impact.
[Bridge]
Miles and years, still the same trick...
Put the picture in their head—quick...
Fog is not mystery; fog is a miss...
Clear image. This.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So seven mornings: telepathy drill—one clear image, two minutes flat,
Stephen King's law as the mat
You practice on. Writing is mind-to-mind if you transmit clean,
Not if you preen.
Tomorrow: describe one real object so a stranger draws it right,
Then use that skill on the page at night.
No abstraction for the first paragraph—earn the idea after the sight,
That's how telepathy lights.
Transmit clear images. Check the receive. Adjust the send—
Mind to mind. Amen. The end.
